nativeRange.habitat: Standing or very slow-moving freshwater across the Amazon basin and Central America — ponds, oxbow lakes, river backwaters, flooded forest margins, and quiet ditches; floats freely on warm, nutrient-rich tropical surface water with full or partial sun

decorPreferences: open, calm water surface for floating rosettes to spread
low to moderate surface flow — strong agitation or spray bars wet the upper leaves and trigger melt
open-top tank or a lid with a sizeable air gap above the water to keep the leaf tops dry and humidity off the crowns
bright overhead lighting kept off the leaf surface — avoid lights mounted so close they scorch the floating rosettes

goodWith: bettas, gouramis and other labyrinth fish that use surface cover for bubble-nesting
shrimp and small fish fry (trailing roots provide grazing surface and shelter)
peaceful tetras, rasboras, livebearers and other community fish
low-tech planted setups with stable nutrients
avoidWith: goldfish, large herbivorous cichlids and other vigorous plant-eaters
tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars or persistent overhead drip
tightly-sealed lids that trap humidity directly against the leaf surface
pond / mystery / nerite snails that have been observed nibbling tender root hairs and emerging leaves

summary: Amazon Frogbit (Limnobium laevigatum) is a fast-growing tropical floating plant with rounded, lily-pad-style leaves and long trailing root tassels. It forms a shaded surface canopy that suppresses algae, provides cover for shrimp and fry, and draws excess nutrients from the water.
careNotes: Amazon Frogbit (Limnobium laevigatum) is the large-leaved tropical counterpart to the cooler-climate European Frogbit (Hydrocharis morsus-ranae). Each plant forms a rosette of smooth, bright-green, oval to nearly circular leaves around 2–5 cm across in dense culture and up to roughly 15 cm wide when given open surface space, sitting on top of the water on a spongy, air-filled lower leaf surface that gives the plant its 'spongeplant' trade name. Long, feathery root tassels hang into the water column and can reach 20–50 cm in a well-fed tank, providing significant grazing and shelter habitat for shrimp and fry. Husbandry is generally considered undemanding: no CO2 is required, fertilization can stay low to moderate (the plant pulls most of its nutrients from fish waste and the water column), and a temperature of 22–26 °C covers the standard tropical community-tank window, with tolerance reported from roughly 18–28 °C. The most common failure mode is leaf melt from persistently wet upper surfaces — tight-fitting lids, condensation, spray bars, or splashing returns will rot the crowns within days — so an open top or a lid with a generous air gap above the water is generally advised. The plant tolerates a wide range of light from low to high, with medium-bright overhead lighting producing the densest growth; intensely close, hot LEDs can scorch the leaf surface.
breedingNotes: Propagates almost exclusively by vegetative runners (stolons) in aquaria — each mature rosette produces lateral shoots that develop into independent daughter plants which can be pinched off and floated separately. Growth rate is typically described as moderate to very fast and a single plant can double its mat surface area within a week or two under bright light and steady nutrient supply, so regular thinning is part of routine maintenance. Sexual reproduction via small white flowers and seed-bearing fruit does occur in the wild and occasionally in open-top setups but is not a practical aquarium propagation route.

primary.name: Tropica (via Aqua Essentials 1-2-GROW! product listing)
primary.notes: Used as a Tropica data-card proxy because the direct Tropica plantdetails URL (https://tropica.com/en/plants/plantdetails/Limnobiumlaevigatum/4711) returned an empty template page at access time. Provides Tropica's stated values: origin South America, growth rate medium, maximum height 5 cm, placement floating, difficulty easy.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Amazon Frogbit Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name and family (Limnobium laevigatum, Hydrocharitaceae), native range (Central and South America, naturalised in Australia, Africa, North America), leaf width (0.5-3 inches, ~1-7.5 cm), root length (up to 50 cm), temperature (18-26 °C), pH (6.0-7.5), water hardness (0-12 °dGH), light (medium-high, 10-12 h photoperiod), CO2 (not required), tank size (≥10 US gal / ~40 L), propagation (fragmentation and plantlets), and compatibility (bettas, gouramis, tetras; avoid plant-eating snails).

additional.name: Glassbox Diaries — Limnobium laevigatum (Amazon Frogbit) Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked leaf height (~5 cm) and leaf width (up to ~15 cm wide on open mats), root length (over 30 cm in a single week), native range (South and Central America), temperature window (18-28 °C, extended tolerance 4-35 °C), pH (5.0-8.0), light (low to high), CO2 (not needed), growth rate (moderate to very fast), placement (floating), substrate (not required), fertilisation (not strictly necessary on a stocked tank), difficulty (easy), and the leaf-melt risk from tightly-sealed lids.

additional.name: Hygronature — Care Guide: Amazon Frogbit
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Amazon River Basin, South America), temperature (18-26 °C / 64-80 °F), water hardness preference (soft to moderately hard), light (moderate to high, adaptable to low), the importance of keeping the leaf tops dry to prevent crown decay, and use as a nutrient-export plant providing shade and shelter.

propagation: vegetative runners / stolons producing daughter rosettes
occasional flower-and-seed sexual reproduction in open-top setups
